author: Michael Grosser
message:
Merge commit from fork

Fix SSRF / file:// LFI via ssrf_filter + opt-in flags (GHSA-9pmc-p236-855h)

author: Jonathan Leitschuh
message:
refactor: Add BUG guards to internal SSRF/LFI helpers (GHSA-9pmc-p236-855h)

`read_local_file` and `fetch_via_net_http` are protected helpers
reachable only from the opt-in branches of `load_uri!` and
`read_remote_file` respectively. A future change that re-routes
either call could silently bypass the LFI / SSRF gate. Add
`raise "BUG: ..."` invariants at the top of each method so any such
regression fails loudly instead of opening the original sinks. The
guards use Ruby's standard "BUG:"-prefixed RuntimeError convention
to signal a programmer-error invariant, not a user-facing condition.

author: Jonathan Leitschuh
message:
vuln-fix: Route remote fetches via ssrf_filter; gate file:// (GHSA-9pmc-p236-855h)

CssParser::Parser#read_remote_file accepted any host / port / scheme
with no IP filtering and no per-redirect scheme re-validation, so an
HTTP 3xx Location: file://... turned SSRF into arbitrary local file
read. load_uri!('file://...') was a separate local-file-read
primitive under attacker-controllable URIs. Default fetches now
route through SsrfFilter.get; two new off-by-default opt-in flags
(allow_local_network, allow_file_uris) restore each behaviour
independently; file:// is no longer reachable from the remote-fetch
path so the cross-scheme redirect sink is structurally closed.

Weakness: CWE-918 (Server-Side Request Forgery)
Severity: High
CVSS: 7.5
Detection: Manual
Report: https://github.com/premailer/css_parser/security/advisories/GHSA-9pmc-p236-855h
Introduced-in: 4215785aab21c9404b88c0bb6e5d68e7b1a99c8c

Weakness: CWE-73 (External Control of File Name or Path)
Severity: High
CVSS: 7.5
Detection: Manual
Report: https://github.com/premailer/css_parser/security/advisories/GHSA-9pmc-p236-855h
Introduced-in: 4215785aab21c9404b88c0bb6e5d68e7b1a99c8c

Reported-by: Jonathan Leitschuh <jonathan.leitschuh@braze.com>
Signed-off-by: Jonathan Leitschuh <jonathan.leitschuh@braze.com>
Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

author: Jonathan Leitschuh
message:
test: Add failing tests for SSRF and file-URI LFI (GHSA-9pmc-p236-855h)

CssParser::Parser#load_uri! and #read_remote_file accept any host /
scheme / file:// URI with no IP filtering, no per-redirect scheme
re-validation, and no gate on file:// via load_uri!. The new tests
pin the desired post-fix behaviour: two independent off-by-default
opt-in flags (allow_local_network, allow_file_uris) gate the SSRF
and LFI paths respectively, and a pair of cross-tests asserts that
enabling one does not enable the other. Assertions are made at the
socket layer (TCPSocket / Socket.tcp) and at File.read so they
survive future HTTP-library swaps. All assertions fail against
unfixed code.

Weakness: CWE-918 (Server-Side Request Forgery)
Severity: High
Weakness: CWE-73 (External Control of File Name or Path)
Severity: High
Detection: Manual
Report: https://github.com/premailer/css_parser/security/advisories/GHSA-9pmc-p236-855h

Reported-by: Jonathan Leitschuh <jonathan.leitschuh@braze.com>
Signed-off-by: Jonathan Leitschuh <jonathan.leitschuh@braze.com>
Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

author: Michael Grosser
message:
Merge pull request #187 from fukayatsu/fix-rgba-leading-dot-alpha

Accept CSS numbers without a leading zero in rgb/rgba/hsl/hsla

author: fukayatsu
message:
Accept CSS numbers without a leading zero in rgb/rgba/hsl/hsla

The numeric colour regexps used `\d+(\.\d+)?` which requires at least
one digit before the decimal point, so values like `rgba(0,0,0,.1)`
were not recognised as colours. Per CSS Values & Units, `<number>`
permits the integer part to be omitted (`[0-9]* '.' [0-9]+`).

The practical impact is that shorthand expansion silently dropped the
colour:

  border: 1px solid rgba(0,0,0,.1)
    -> border-top-width / -style are kept, border-top-color is gone.

  background: url(x.png) rgba(0,0,0,.1)
    -> background-image is kept, background-color is gone.

This bites users of Dart Sass + Premailer because Dart Sass's
`compressed` output strips leading zeros, so `.1` is what the
inliner actually receives.

Switching the integer-and-optional-fraction pattern to
`(?:\d*\.)?\d+` accepts `1`, `1.5`, and `.5` while still
rejecting a bare `1.` (also invalid per spec).

Tests cover both the regex itself (positive cases for `.1`-style
values) and the shorthand-expansion regression for `border:` and
`background:`.

Amp-Thread-ID: https://ampcode.com/threads/T-019c8b1f-829d-70cd-83c5-3409a635e616
Co-authored-by: Amp <amp@ampcode.com>

author: Paul Sokolik
message:
Fix process-global memory leak in folded_declaration_cache

In long-lived processes (e.g., Sidekiq workers rendering emails via
Premailer), the process-global `@folded_declaration_cache` on
`CssParser::Parser` accumulates entries from every CSS parse operation
and is never cleared, causing unbounded memory growth.

The class-level `@folded_declaration_cache` (and its `attr_reader`) on
`CssParser::Parser` was defined as a class instance variable that
persists for the lifetime of the process. While `reset!` (called in
`initialize`) creates a per-instance `@folded_declaration_cache`, the
class-level variable and accessor remained, leaking the global
reference.

Similarly, `CssParser.merge` set a module-level
`@folded_declaration_cache = {}` on every call, which also persisted
at the module level.

This commit:
- Removes the class-level `@folded_declaration_cache` and its
  `class << self; attr_reader` from `CssParser::Parser`
- Removes the module-level `@folded_declaration_cache` assignment
  in `CssParser.merge`
- The per-instance cache (set in `reset!`) is already properly scoped
  and gets garbage collected with the `Parser` instance
- Adds tests verifying the cache is per-instance and not class-level

Amp-Thread-ID: https://ampcode.com/threads/T-019c8b1f-829d-70cd-83c5-3409a635e616
Co-authored-by: Amp <amp@ampcode.com>

author: Michael Grosser
message:
Merge pull request #175 from mogest/master

Prefer !important rules over non-!important rules in the same ruleset

author: Mog Nesbitt
message:
Prefer !important rules over non-!important rules in the same ruleset

Previously, the css rule "color: black !important; color: red;" would be
collapsed to "color: red;" as the processor did not take whether the
!important flag was set inside the same rule.

This commit makes the Declaration Value initializer take notice of the
!important flag and correctly handle it.
